theorem_statement:
Banach-Stone Theorem for real-valued continuous functions.

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be compact Hausdorff spaces. Suppose
\[
T : C(X,\mathbb{R}) \to C(Y,\mathbb{R})
\]
is a surjective linear isometry, where both spaces are equipped with the supremum norm. Then there exist:
- a homeomorphism \(\tau : Y \to X\), and
- a continuous function \(h : Y \to \{-1,1\}\),

such that for every \(f \in C(X,\mathbb{R})\) and every \(y \in Y\),
\[
(Tf)(y) = h(y)\, f(\tau(y)).
\]
In particular, \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic.
raw_proof_text:
We prove the Banach-Stone theorem for real-valued continuous functions on compact Hausdorff spaces.

Let
\[
T : C(X,\mathbb{R}) \to C(Y,\mathbb{R})
\]
be a surjective linear isometry, where \(X\) and \(Y\) are compact Hausdorff spaces and both function spaces carry the supremum norm.

Our goal is to show that there exist a homeomorphism \(\tau : Y \to X\) and a continuous sign function \(h : Y \to \{-1,1\}\) such that
\[
(Tf)(y) = h(y) f(\tau(y))
\qquad\text{for all } f \in C(X,\mathbb{R}),\ y \in Y.
\]
Once this is proved, it follows immediately that \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic.

## Step 1: the adjoint preserves the unit ball and its extreme points

Let
\[
T^* : C(Y,\mathbb{R})^* \to C(X,\mathbb{R})^*
\]
be the adjoint map, defined by
\[
(T^*\mu)(f) := \mu(Tf)
\qquad\text{for } \mu \in C(Y,\mathbb{R})^*,\ f \in C(X,\mathbb{R}).
\]
Since \(T\) is a surjective linear isometry, it is an isometric isomorphism of Banach spaces. Therefore its adjoint \(T^*\) is also a linear isometric isomorphism between the dual spaces. In particular, \(T^*\) maps the closed unit ball of \(C(Y,\mathbb{R})^*\) bijectively onto the closed unit ball of \(C(X,\mathbb{R})^*\).

Any affine bijection between compact convex sets sends extreme points to extreme points. Hence \(T^*\) sends extreme points of the dual unit ball of \(C(Y,\mathbb{R})\) to extreme points of the dual unit ball of \(C(X,\mathbb{R})\).

## Step 2: identify the extreme points of the dual unit ball

By the Riesz representation theorem, every continuous linear functional on \(C(X,\mathbb{R})\) is represented by a finite signed regular Borel measure on \(X\). For each point \(x\in X\), let \(\delta_x\) denote the evaluation functional
\[
\delta_x(f) = f(x).
\]
Its norm is \(1\), and so does the norm of \(-\delta_x\).

A standard fact for the real-valued Banach-Stone theorem is that the extreme points of the unit ball of \(C(X,\mathbb{R})^*\) are exactly the functionals
\[
\pm \delta_x \, , \qquad x\in X.
\]
Likewise, the extreme points of the unit ball of \(C(Y,\mathbb{R})^*\) are exactly the functionals
\[
\pm \delta_y \, , \qquad y\in Y.
\]

Therefore, for each \(y\in Y\), the extreme point \(\delta_y\) of the unit ball of \(C(Y,\mathbb{R})^*\) is sent by \(T^*\) to an extreme point of the unit ball of \(C(X,\mathbb{R})^*\). Hence there exist a point \(\tau(y)\in X\) and a sign \(h(y)\in\{-1,1\}\) such that
\[
T^*\delta_y = h(y)\, \delta_{\tau(y)}.
\]
Equivalently, for every \(f\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\),
\[
(Tf)(y) = \delta_y(Tf) = (T^*\delta_y)(f) = h(y)\,f(\tau(y)).
\]
Thus we have already obtained the pointwise representation
\[
(Tf)(y)=h(y)f(\tau(y)).
\]
It remains to prove that \(h\) is continuous and that \(\tau\) is a homeomorphism.

## Step 3: continuity of the sign function \(h\)

Apply the representation formula to the constant function \(1\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\). Since \(1(\tau(y))=1\), we get
\[
(T1)(y)=h(y)
\qquad\text{for all } y\in Y.
\]
But \(T1\in C(Y,\mathbb{R})\), so \(h=T1\) is continuous.

Because the codomain \(\{-1,1\}\) is discrete, this means \(h\) is locally constant.

## Step 4: continuity of \(\tau\)

We now prove that \(\tau : Y\to X\) is continuous.

Let \((y_\alpha)\) be a net in \(Y\) converging to \(y\). We must show that
\[
\tau(y_\alpha) \to \tau(y)
\]
in \(X\).

Take any function \(f\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\). Since \(Tf\in C(Y,\mathbb{R})\), we have
\[
(Tf)(y_\alpha) \to (Tf)(y).
\]
Using the representation formula,
\[
h(y_\alpha) f(\tau(y_\alpha)) \to h(y) f(\tau(y)).
\]
But we already know that \(h\) is continuous, so
\[
h(y_\alpha) \to h(y).
\]
Since each \(h(y_\alpha)\) and \(h(y)\) is either \(1\) or \(-1\), eventually we must have
\[
h(y_\alpha)=h(y).
\]
Therefore for all sufficiently large \(\alpha\),
\[
f(\tau(y_\alpha)) = h(y)^{-1}(Tf)(y_\alpha).
\]
Passing to the limit gives
\[
f(\tau(y_\alpha)) \to f(\tau(y)).
\]
Thus every continuous real-valued function on \(X\) takes convergent values along the net \((\tau(y_\alpha))\), with limit equal to its value at \(\tau(y)\).

Since \(X\) is compact Hausdorff, continuous real-valued functions separate points and determine the topology. Hence this implies
\[
\tau(y_\alpha) \to \tau(y).
\]
So \(\tau\) is continuous.

## Step 5: construct the inverse map

Because \(T\) is a surjective linear isometry, its inverse
\[
T^{-1} : C(Y,\mathbb{R}) \to C(X,\mathbb{R})
\]
is also a surjective linear isometry. Applying the same argument to \(T^{-1}\), we obtain a continuous function
\[
k : X \to \{-1,1\}
\]
and a map
\[
\sigma : X \to Y
\]
such that for every \(g\in C(Y,\mathbb{R})\) and every \(x\in X\),
\[
(T^{-1}g)(x) = k(x) g(\sigma(x)).
\]
We will show that \(\sigma\) is the inverse of \(\tau\).

## Step 6: prove that \(\tau\) and \(\sigma\) are inverse maps

Let \(f\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\) and \(x\in X\). Since \(T^{-1}(Tf)=f\), we have
\[
f(x) = (T^{-1}(Tf))(x).
\]
Using the representation formula for \(T^{-1}\),
\[
f(x) = k(x)\,(Tf)(\sigma(x)).
\]
Now use the representation formula for \(T\):
\[
(Tf)(\sigma(x)) = h(\sigma(x))\, f(\tau(\sigma(x))).
\]
Thus
\[
f(x) = k(x) h(\sigma(x))\, f(\tau(\sigma(x))).
\]
This holds for every \(f\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\). Since continuous real-valued functions separate points of the compact Hausdorff space \(X\), the only way this identity can hold for all \(f\) is if
\[
\tau(\sigma(x)) = x.
\]
Indeed, if \(\tau(\sigma(x))\neq x\), there would exist some \(f\in C(X,\mathbb{R})\) with different values at those two points, contradicting the formula above.

So
\[
\tau\circ\sigma = \operatorname{id}_X.
\]
By the same argument, applying the same reasoning to \(T\circ T^{-1} = \operatorname{id}_{C(Y,\mathbb{R})}\), we obtain
\[
\sigma\circ\tau = \operatorname{id}_Y.
\]
Therefore \(\tau\) and \(\sigma\) are inverse bijections.

Since \(\tau\) is continuous and bijective with continuous inverse \(\sigma\), it follows that \(\tau\) is a homeomorphism.

## Step 7: final representation

We have shown that there exist a homeomorphism \(\tau : Y\to X\) and a continuous sign function \(h : Y\to\{-1,1\}\) such that
\[
(Tf)(y) = h(y)\,f(\tau(y))
\qquad\text{for all } f\in C(X,\mathbb{R}),\ y\in Y.
\]
In particular, \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic.

This completes the proof.
